editableNewDialogue.text: When a regex uses `.{1,6}`, does it include spaces and punctuation too?
Yes—`.` matches almost any character, including spaces and punctuation. The common exception is newline (`\n`) unless you enable a “dotall” mode (often `s`), which makes `.` match newlines too.
In sed, is there a way to match hex like `0x1A2b` case-insensitively?

If your `sed` supports ERE (`sed -E`), you can do something like: `0x[0-9A-Fa-f]+`. Classic `sed` doesn’t have a global case-insensitive flag for regex itself, so you typically spell out both ranges as shown.
What’s the difference between formatting and deobfuscation for JavaScript?

Formatting (beautifying) mostly adds whitespace/indentation and line breaks so structure is easier to see. Deobfuscation tries to reverse transformations like string arrays, control-flow flattening, or renamed identifiers to recover clearer logic—often requiring partial evaluation or rewriting, not just reformatting.
Random aside: I saw a note that early 2000s browsers handled regex differently—true?

Somewhat. Old JS engines (and some older tools) had quirks or incomplete regex features compared to modern ECMAScript—things like edge-case behavior, performance pitfalls, and missing features (e.g., lookbehind came much later). Specs and implementations have converged a lot since then.

Those deobfuscators are helpful, but I’m stuck with a file that uses the old `sed` syntax—no `-r` and no `-E` on this box. Any way to do the hex `0x...` to decimal replacement without relying on extended regex?

Yes—stick to basic regex and use a loop plus `printf` via `sh`/`awk` (since plain sed can’t convert bases by itself). One common approach is to have `sed` locate `0x[0-9A-Fa-f]+`, then hand each match off to something that can do base conversion (e.g., `awk`’s `strtonum` in gawk, or `printf "%d"` in a shell with arithmetic). If you tell me what Unix you’re on, I can tailor the command to tools available there.
Quick correction on that music bit: I’m not really into A Place to Bury Strangers or The Black Angels—I just needed similar-band names for a playlist I was compiling for someone else.
Got it—so that request was for research/curation, not your personal taste. I won’t treat it as your own long-term preference.
